title: Tapping for the Good Mother Pressure
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the gap between the mother you are and the one you feel you should be
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the pressure of the good-mother standard: always available, endlessly patient, never depleted. Taps through the guilt, the anger at the standard itself, and toward a clearer relationship with what mothering actually requires.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the gap between the mother you are and the mother you feel you should be is generating guilt, shame, or exhaustion.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the pressure of the good-mother standard: the standard that is never fully defined, never met, and never goes away. This sequence works on the gap between the mother you are and the one you feel you should be, and on the exhaustion of trying to close that gap without ever being told what the target actually is.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I am not the mother I feel I should be and that gap is painful, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I'm exhausted by the pressure and don't know how to put it down, I accept this is where I am right now.
Even though I feel guilt I can't always name or justify, I'm willing to look at what's underneath it.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: I should be doing more.
Side of Eye: Other mothers seem to manage what I can't.
Under Eye: The guilt when I need time away from them.
Under Nose: The anger I feel that I'm not allowed to admit.
Chin: The performance of good-mothering and how exhausting it is.
Collarbone: The distance between who I am and what I think I should be.
Under Arm: The guilt that never fully goes away.
Top of Head: The standard I can't fully see but feel failing every day.
Reframe
Eyebrow: My children need a real mother, not a performance of one.
Side of Eye: I cannot see what other mothers are carrying. The comparison is incomplete.
Under Eye: Needing time for myself is not abandonment. It is maintenance.
Under Nose: The anger is information. It is telling me something is out of balance.
Chin: The standard I'm trying to meet is not a standard anyone agreed to. It came from somewhere and it can be questioned.
Collarbone: My ordinary mothering, the repetitive and imperfect daily kind, is the work.
Under Arm: The guilt can be a signal, not a verdict.
Top of Head: I am a real mother. That is what they need.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
